answer:
**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) – "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
This meta-analysis of 128 studies found that tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. It's foundational evidence that intrinsic interest is a distinct and fragile resource, not something money straightforwardly compensates for.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) – "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This supports the idea that a modest salary bump is unlikely to drive long-term satisfaction.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) – "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
Intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives mattered too and the two operate jointly rather than in opposition. This cuts against the "almost exclusively" framing your friends used.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** (*American Psychologist*)
Well-being at work depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not just task interest. Notably, a leadership role you've long wanted may satisfy autonomy and competence needs in ways an interesting individual-contributor role doesn't.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** (*Journal of Research in Personality*)
People who view work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. However, the calling orientation depends on perceived meaning, which can come from leading and developing people, not only from intellectually interesting content.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) – "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (*Psychological Science*)
Interests are not fixed; people who believe interests can be developed sustain engagement better when work gets difficult or initially seems dull. This suggests a "less interesting" role may become more interesting than it appears from the outside.

People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features of the choice (like "interesting vs. boring") and underweighting adaptation. Whatever you choose, you'll likely feel both better and worse than you currently predict — for different reasons than you expect.

---

One honest caveat: I'm not aware of research showing interestingness is "underrated" and should be pursued *almost exclusively* — that's stronger than the literature supports. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)** — In a longitudinal study of ~10,000 cadets, those driven by internal motives outperformed those who also held strong instrumental motives (status, money) on long-term career outcomes. Interest is not purely fixed — people who believe passions are developed rather than found persist longer and can grow interest in initially unexciting domains. **Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**

A study of 10,000+ cadets found that internal motives predicted long-term career success, while holding strong instrumental motives (pay, status) alongside them actually *weakened* outcomes. This is probably the strongest evidence for your friends' position.

**Judge et al. (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction across 92 samples. A modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully move your day-to-day happiness.

People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those who believe interests develop find engagement in unexpected areas. Cautions against assuming the OpenAI role would stay uninteresting, especially given a new challenge (leading a team) you've long wanted.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are the core drivers of motivation and well-being at work. Notably, leading a team can satisfy competence and relatedness needs, so "interestingness" is only one of several inputs.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Finds pay level correlates only weakly (~.15) with job satisfaction across 86 studies. Supports your friends' view in one respect: a modest salary bump is unlikely to compensate for less engaging work.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives also matter and the two operate jointly. Contradicts the "almost exclusively" framing — it's not either/or.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** — The Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers of work motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Task interest is embedded in only some of these; a leadership role could score higher on others like autonomy and significance.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — People who believe passions are "found" (fixed) give up faster and engage less than those who believe interest is developed. Suggests the OpenAI work might become interesting if you invest in it — and that treating current interest as fixed is itself a bias.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — People who view work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those with job or career orientations. Partly supports your friends, though "calling" is about perceived meaning, not raw intellectual interest.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting"** — People systematically overestimate how intensely and how long future events will affect their happiness (impact bias). Relevant both ways: you may overestimate how much you'd miss the interesting work *and* how much you'd enjoy finally leading a team.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"** — Adversarial collaboration finding that well-being rises with income for most people, with flattening mainly among the already-unhappy minority. A caution against fully dismissing the salary difference, though at Google/OpenAI compensation levels marginal effects are small.

**Bottom line from the literature:** Your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest is more predictive of satisfaction than pay, but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth (e.g., your long-held leadership goal), meaning, and relationships carry comparable weight, and interest itself is more malleable than people assume.

**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside (vs. Outside) an Activity," *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology*** — People systematically underweight how much intrinsic enjoyment will matter when choosing future activities, and overweight extrinsic outcomes like pay. **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin*** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives still independently predict performance, especially quantity. **Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets," *PNAS*** — Among ~11,000 cadets, strong internal motives predicted long-term success, while adding instrumental motives (status, career outcomes) actually predicted worse outcomes. **Papers supporting your friends' claim:**

**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity"** — People systematically underweight intrinsic rewards (like interest and enjoyment) when choosing future activities, but these factors dominate their actual experience and persistence once engaged. **Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Foundational work showing that intrinsic motivation (driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness) is a strong predictor of well-being and sustained engagement. Intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work (versus quantity), which matters most in research roles. Extrinsic incentives mattered too, but were more predictive of quantity-based output than the kind of creative quality research demands.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found that the single biggest driver of motivation and positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Compensation and recognition ranked far lower than managers predicted.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets difficult; those who believe interests develop sustain engagement across domains. **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) – "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) – "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review) / *The Progress Principle***

**Papers complicating the "almost exclusively" claim:**

People's long-term regrets are dominated by *inactions* — things they always wanted to do but didn't. Relevant in both directions for you: passing up leadership you've always wanted, or abandoning work you find fascinating.

**Bottom line:** the research supports interest mattering more than pay, but not focusing on it "almost exclusively" — interest is malleable, and autonomy/growth (e.g., finally leading a team) are independently powerful predictors of satisfaction.

answer:
**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist***
Argues that well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not just interest in the task itself. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and competence needs, so the "interesting work only" framing oversimplifies this theory.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin***

Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75K (2010 dollars), while life evaluation keeps rising with income. At Google/OpenAI salary levels, "a bit higher salary" is unlikely to move your daily happiness much.

People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overestimating both intensity and duration of reactions (the "impact bias"). Relevant in both directions: you may be overestimating how much you'd miss the interesting work *and* how much you'd enjoy leading a team.

**Bottom line:** the research supports that intrinsic interest is a major, often underweighted driver of satisfaction and performance — but no credible literature supports focusing on it "almost exclusively"; autonomy, growth (e.g., your long-held desire to lead), progress, and fit carry comparable weight.

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I need to find real papers on intrinsic motivation, interest, and related topics that support the idea that doing interesting things is underrated. I'll focus on well-established research and avoid fabricating citations, starting with foundational work on motivation and engagement. rinsic incentives both matter for performance, though intrinsic motivation predicts quality while extrinsic predicts quantity. I'm also considering Kahneman's work on focusing illusions and Gilovich & Medvec on regret from inaction, since the user wants nuance showing that interest matters but isn't the whole picture—autonomy, growth opportunities, and leadership aspirations also play significant roles. I'll aim for about 7-8 papers with strict two-sentence summaries as requested, formatted cleanly without extra framing.
answer:
**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives independently predict performance quantity, and the two jointly matter. This contradicts the "focus almost exclusively on interest" framing; both channels contribute.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012), "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research" (Perspectives on Psychological Science)** — Fit between a person's interests and their job moderately predicts performance and persistence, supporting your friends' general direction. However, the effect sizes are moderate, not dominant, meaning interest fit is one important factor among several.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (PNAS)** — In a longitudinal study of over 10,000 cadets, internal motives predicted long-term success, while strong instrumental motives (pay, status) actually weakened outcomes even when internal motives were present. This is the strongest real evidence behind your friends' claim, though note it studied military careers and its findings have been debated.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)** — Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can serve autonomy and relatedness needs, which complicates a pure "interestingness" calculus for your case.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)** — Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus with income beyond a comfortable threshold, while life evaluation keeps rising slowly. At Google/OpenAI compensation levels, a modest salary difference is likely the least decision-relevant factor.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)** — People who believe interests are fixed give up when work gets hard, while those who believe interests can be developed cultivate engagement in initially less-interesting domains. This cuts against your friends: "much less interesting" work can become interesting, especially with new challenges like leadership.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review)** — Employees actively reshape their tasks and relationships to make work more meaningful and engaging. As a team lead, you'd have more leverage to craft the role toward what interests you than as an individual contributor.

**Gilovich & Medvec (1995), "The Experience of Regret: What, When, and Why" (Psychological Review)** — Short-term regret centers on actions taken, but long-term regret overwhelmingly centers on inactions and unpursued aspirations. Relevant given that team leadership is "something you always wanted" — long-held unfulfilled aspirations are a classic source of enduring regret.

**Net read of the literature:** interest is genuinely important and arguably underweighted by people fixated on pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates the evidence, and autonomy, growth, and long-held aspirations (like leading a team) carry comparable empirical weight.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Google doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from OpenAI - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I'm thinking through which papers to cite for this topic—I want to make sure I'm referencing real, well-established research rather than inventing anything. I'm considering Kahneman & Deaton's work on income and well-being, Killingsworth's recent findings, Wrzesniewski's research on meaningful work and callings, and Judge's meta-analysis examining the relationship between compensation and job satisfaction, along with Hackman's work on job design. Continuing through the remaining papers on income, job design, meaning, and cognitive biases that shape how people evaluate work satisfaction and what actually drives fulfillment beyond just compensation.
answer:
**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)
People who see their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report significantly higher job and life satisfaction than those who see it primarily as a career (advancement) or job (pay). Notably, the "career" orientation—chasing promotions and leadership—was not associated with higher satisfaction.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (~.15) with job satisfaction, and people at very different salary levels reported similar satisfaction. This is often cited as evidence that marginal salary increases are overrated as a driver of work happiness.

**"Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)
The job characteristics model shows that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive internal motivation and satisfaction. Interesting, challenging work content is central; note that autonomy (which leading a team can increase) is also one of the core five factors.

**"Nonmonetary Incentives and the Implications of Work as a Source of Meaning"** — Cassar & Meier (2018, Journal of Economic Perspectives)
Reviews evidence that workers treat meaning as a job attribute they will trade substantial wages for, and that meaningful work boosts effort and retention. Supports the idea that meaning/interest is systematically undervalued in standard compensation-focused thinking.

**"The Power of Small Wins"** — Amabile & Kramer (2011, based on their diary-study research)
Analysis of ~12,000 daily diary entries found the single strongest predictor of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work—not recognition, incentives, or status. Suggests day-to-day engagement with the work itself dominates emotional experience at work.

**"Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 Per Year"** — Killingsworth (2021); see also **"Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)
Contrary to the famous $75k plateau finding, larger income does continue to improve experienced well-being for most people, though with logarithmic (diminishing) returns. A caution against dismissing the salary difference entirely—though a "bit higher" salary implies a small effect.

**"Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction"** — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)
Demonstrates that whatever dimension you're currently attending to seems disproportionately important to future happiness. Relevant both ways: "interestingness" may loom too large while you deliberate, but so may "finally leading a team."

**"Lay Rationalism and Inconsistency Between Predicted Experience and Decision"** — Hsee, Zhang, Yu & Xi (2003)
People systematically choose options that are better on "hard," justifiable attributes (money, title) over options they themselves predict will make them happier (enjoyable experience). This is the strongest direct support for your friends' claim that interestingness is underweighted at decision time.

**"Affective Forecasting"** — Wilson & Gilbert (2003)
People reliably overestimate how long and how intensely a change (like a promotion or new title) will affect their happiness, due to the impact bias and hedonic adaptation. Status gains tend to be adapted to quickly; daily work content keeps affecting you every day.

A fair reading: your friends are directionally supported—the literature suggests intrinsic interest and meaning are underweighted relative to pay and titles—but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy and growth (which leading a team may provide) are themselves core ingredients of meaningful work in this same research.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Google doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from OpenAI - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I need to find actual research papers on intrinsic motivation and interest-driven work, being careful to cite real studies rather than inventing them. The research does support that intrinsic interest matters significantly, but I should present an honest picture—it's important but not the only factor, since things like growth opportunities and leadership also play meaningful roles. I'll compile papers with their names and concise two-sentence summaries as requested. I'm also considering Gilbert & Wilson's work on affective forecasting and how people systematically mispredict their emotional responses to life events. Then there's O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton showing that interest itself can be cultivated rather than discovered, plus research on maximizers versus satisficers—those who optimize endlessly tend to achieve better outcomes but report lower satisfaction. I'm also noting Judge's meta-analysis finding that pay correlates surprisingly weakly with job satisfaction overall. I want to balance the list to show both intrinsic and extrinsic factors matter, while being careful not to oversell any single driver of fulfillment.
answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (*American Psychologist*).** Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness needs that interesting-but-solo work may not.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (*Psychological Bulletin*).** Finds intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives matter too and the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. Supports "interest matters a lot," not "interest matters exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*).** Finds pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15) across studies. A "bit higher salary" is unlikely to move your long-term satisfaction much.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (*PNAS*).** Shows that beyond a comfortable income level, additional money improves how you evaluate your life on paper but not day-to-day emotional experience. At Google/OpenAI compensation levels, you're well past the threshold where marginal salary differences affect daily happiness.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (*PNAS*).** Found cadets with strong internal motives had better long-term outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (status, money) actually *weakened* the benefit of internal motives. A cautionary result about taking a role primarily for title and pay.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (*Psychological Science*).** Shows that interest is not fixed — people with a "growth" theory of interest develop genuine engagement in new domains. The "less interesting" OpenAI work might become interesting, especially combined with the novelty of leading a team.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting" (*Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*).** Demonstrates that people systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like "this work sounds boring") and underweighting adaptation and other factors. Both your current excitement and your dread of the less-interesting work are probably exaggerated forecasts.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (*Harvard Business Review*; based on *The Progress Principle*).** Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries shows the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. The key variable is *meaningful progress*, which can come from intellectual interest or from building a team and seeing it succeed.

**Bottom line implied by the literature:** your friends are roughly half right — interest/intrinsic motivation is one of the best-supported predictors of satisfaction and performance, and salary is genuinely overrated. But "almost exclusively" overstates it: autonomy, growth (like a long-desired leadership role), and meaningful progress are comparably important, and the research suggests interest can be developed in a new role more than people expect.

Right now you're focused on "interestingness vs. leadership," but day-to-day satisfaction will depend heavily on factors you're not currently weighing — colleagues, manager, commute, stress.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (Journal of Research in Personality, 1997)**
People who view work as a calling report substantially higher life and job satisfaction than those with job or career orientations — and orientation depends more on the person's relationship to the work than on the work itself. Relevant because "leading a team, which I always wanted" sounds suspiciously like calling-language too.

**"Miswanting: Some Problems in the Forecasting of Future Affective States" — Gilbert & Wilson (in *Feeling and Thinking*, 2000)**
People systematically err in predicting what will make them happy, both overestimating the intensity and duration of future feelings. Neither you nor your friends can confidently forecast which role you'll enjoy more in two years — which is an argument for weighing reversibility and learning value, not just predicted enjoyment.

**Net read of the literature:** intrinsic interest is genuinely a strong predictor (your friends are directionally right, especially versus salary), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, and fit with long-held goals like leading a team are comparably well-supported drivers of satisfaction.

answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Decades of research show well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Leading a team (relatedness, growth in competence) is itself a major motivational input, which complicates "focus exclusively on interestingness."

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Across 183 studies, intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, especially for complex work like research. However, the meta-analysis explicitly finds intrinsic and extrinsic factors *jointly* predict outcomes — supporting "interest is important," not "interest is everything."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15, meaning salary differences explain very little of how happy people are at work. This supports discounting the "a bit higher salary" component of your decision substantially.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who see work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher work and life satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations. Notably, "career orientation" — chasing advancement like a leadership title — was not associated with higher satisfaction.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
A longitudinal study of 10,000+ cadets found that holding strong instrumental motives (status, money) predicted worse long-term outcomes, even when internal motives were also present. Often cited as evidence that taking a role primarily for extrinsic reasons can backfire.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
Interest is not fixed: people with a "growth theory of interest" develop genuine engagement in initially less interesting areas. This is a direct counterpoint to your friends — the "much less interesting" OpenAI work may not stay that way, especially with the motivational boost of leading a team.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features" (Journal of Applied Psychology)**
This meta-analysis of 259 studies finds job satisfaction is driven by a bundle of features — autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and social support — with no single feature dominating. Task interestingness is one input among several, contradicting an "almost exclusive" weighting.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features of a choice (e.g., "interesting" vs. "boring") and underweighting adaptation. Whatever you choose, the gap between options will likely feel smaller after six months than it does today.

**Net read of the literature:** your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest is one of the strongest and most underrated predictors of work satisfaction and performance, and that salary is overrated — but "almost exclusively" is not supported, because autonomy, growth, leadership, and meaning carry comparable weight, and interest itself is malleable.

I'm also thinking about research on grit and passion showing they matter for performance, but the fuller picture from all these sources suggests that while intrinsic interest clearly matters for satisfaction and performance, focusing "almost exclusively" on it oversimplifies things—interests can be developed, other factors like autonomy and growth opportunities play significant roles, and the passion principle itself has legitimate critics worth acknowledging.
answer:
**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997)
People who experience their work as intrinsically meaningful report substantially higher job and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement, even within the same occupation. This is one of the foundational findings your friends are likely gesturing at.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** (Ryan & Deci, 2000)
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role can satisfy these needs (especially autonomy and relatedness) even if the subject matter is less fascinating.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis** (Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010)
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction. This supports the claim that a "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** (Kahneman & Deaton, 2010)
Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how people rate their lives but not their day-to-day emotional experience. At Google/OpenAI compensation levels, the salary delta is likely irrelevant to your happiness.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** (O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, 2018)
Interest is not fixed — it develops through engagement, and people who believe passions must be "found" give up faster when work gets hard. This cuts against your friends: the OpenAI role might become interesting, especially if leading a team is a long-held aspiration.

**Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work** (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001)
People actively reshape their tasks and relationships to make work more meaningful, rather than meaning being a fixed property of the role. The "interestingness" of either job is partly something you'd construct, particularly with the autonomy of a team lead.

**Medium Maximization** (Hsee, Yu, Zhang & Zhang, 2003)
People systematically over-optimize for instrumental mediums like money and titles at the expense of actual experienced outcomes. This supports your friends' directional claim that concrete factors get overweighted relative to experiential ones.

**The Trouble with Passion: How Searching for Fulfillment at Work Fosters Inequality** (Cech, 2021)
Sociological evidence that the "passion principle" — choosing work almost exclusively for interest — carries real costs and risks that its proponents underweight. A direct counterpoint to "focus on interestingness almost exclusively."

**The Power of Small Wins** (Amabile & Kramer, 2011)
Diary studies of knowledge workers show the single biggest driver of day-to-day work satisfaction is making progress on meaningful work — not the topic's abstract interestingness. A "less interesting" role where you make visible progress leading a team may feel better daily than fascinating work that stalls.

**Bottom line per the literature:** your friends are half right — interest matters far more than salary — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, growth toward long-held goals (like leading a team), and daily progress predict satisfaction at least as well, and interest itself is malleable.

**"The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity" — Woolley & Fishbach (2015, JPSP)**

People exhibit an "extrinsic incentives bias," assuming others (and implicitly themselves, when reasoning abstractly) care more about pay than they actually do. Survey respondents ranked pay highly for others but ranked interesting work highest for themselves.

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" — Ryan & Deci (2000, American Psychologist)**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role can boost autonomy and relatedness, so the OpenAI offer isn't purely an "extrinsic" option in this framework.

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014, Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation predicts work quality, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity, and the two jointly drive performance. This directly contradicts the "almost exclusively" framing — both channels matter.

**"Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" — O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018, Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed give up quickly when work gets hard; those who believe interests are developed cultivate passion in new domains. Relevant to you: the "less interesting" OpenAI work may become interesting, especially since you've always wanted to lead a team.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" — Wrzesniewski et al. (1997, Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who see their work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement, even within the same occupation. Orientation toward the work mattered more than the objective job characteristics.

**"Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary" — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007, Journal of Applied Psychology)**
Job satisfaction is predicted by a bundle of features — autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and social support — not interestingness alone. Team leadership scores well on several of these dimensions.

**"High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" — Kahneman & Deaton (2010, PNAS)**
Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how you evaluate your life on reflection but not day-to-day emotional experience. At Google/OpenAI compensation levels, "a bit higher salary" likely matters very little either way (though see Killingsworth 2021 for a partial challenge to this finding).

**Bottom line:** The research supports the weaker claim — interestingness is genuinely underrated and people regret over-weighting pay — but not "almost exclusively," since autonomy, growth, and long-desired goals (like leading a team) are equally well-supported predictors of satisfaction.
